content.assessment: A transparency and registry framework is comparatively low in legal-authority demands since it does not mandate any particular maintainer or closure model, but it still requires statutory or administrative authority to compel standardized reporting from school district centers and to resolve ambiguities such as the transport-duty assignment that currently undermine consistent cost accounting. Its accountability value depends on whether reporting requirements are binding rather than advisory, given the historical pattern of district councils' advisory-only role and of informal, non-codified reversal of decisions.
opportunities: Reported 2026 restructuring plans aimed at addressing daily operational dysfunction in the Klebelsberg Center/district system could provide a natural legislative vehicle for embedding registry and transparency requirements (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)

A registry could formalize accountability for the currently informal political-intervention reversal channel by providing documented evidence trails that support or contest such interventions (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
strengths: Directly addresses the documented ambiguity in transport-obligation assignment by proposing standardized, transparent procedural rules independent of operational model, which would clarify accountability regardless of which reform path is chosen (F-live-legal_and_governance-05)

As a model-agnostic infrastructure, it avoids requiring wholesale restructuring of maintainer authority, reducing the scope of legal change needed relative to consolidation or networked-retention proposals
threats: Interior Ministry oversight of education, rather than an education-specialized authority, may deprioritize education-outcome transparency relative to other governance concerns (F-live-legal_and_governance-03)

Absent statutory compulsion, data reporting by tankerület centers could be inconsistent or unaudited, replicating the same discretionary opacity documented in current closure practice (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
weaknesses: A purely informational registry lacks enforceability unless paired with binding decision rules, and the current governance structure demonstrates a pattern of advisory bodies (district councils) being overridden by centralized administrative discretion (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

No existing statutory mechanism currently requires district centers to report standardized cost or outcome data, so implementation would depend on new administrative mandate authority not addressed by current law
